questions.q: The Peacock Throne was a famous ceremonial throne of which empire?
questions.explanation: The Peacock Throne was the celebrated jewelled throne of the Mughal emperors.

questions.q: Which Mughal emperor commissioned the Peacock Throne?
questions.explanation: The emperor Shah Jahan commissioned the lavish Peacock Throne.

questions.q: The Peacock Throne was especially famous for its lavish use of what?
questions.explanation: The throne was renowned for its extravagant use of gold and precious gemstones.

questions.q: Which foreign invader carried off the Peacock Throne from Delhi?
questions.explanation: The Persian ruler Nadir Shah carried off the Peacock Throne after sacking Delhi.

questions.q: In what year was the Peacock Throne taken from the Mughals by Nadir Shah?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah seized the Peacock Throne when he invaded and sacked Delhi in 1739.

questions.q: Which country did Nadir Shah, who took the Peacock Throne, rule?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah was the ruler of Persia (Iran) when he invaded Mughal India in 1739.

questions.q: The throne took its name from the figures of which bird that decorated it?
questions.explanation: The throne was named for the jewelled peacock figures that adorned it.

questions.q: Along with the Peacock Throne, Nadir Shah carried off which famous diamond?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah took the Koh-i-Noor diamond along with the Peacock Throne and much imperial treasure.

questions.q: The Peacock Throne is best described as which kind of object?
questions.explanation: It was a jewelled ceremonial throne, a symbol of Mughal imperial splendour.

questions.q: The loss of the Peacock Throne is associated with which broader process in Mughal history?
options: The empire's expansion into the Deccan
questions.explanation: Its loss in 1739 was a symbol of the accelerating decline of the Mughal Empire.

questions.q: In which Mughal capital was the Peacock Throne installed in its great audience hall?
questions.explanation: The throne stood in the Diwan-i-Khas of Shahjahanabad, Shah Jahan's new capital at Delhi.

questions.q: The Persian name often used for the Peacock Throne was which?
questions.explanation: In Persian the throne was called the Takht-i-Taus, meaning 'Peacock Throne'.

questions.q: Roughly how many years did it reportedly take to construct the original Peacock Throne for Shah Jahan?
questions.explanation: Accounts say the original Peacock Throne took about seven years to complete for Shah Jahan.

questions.q: Which European jeweller-traveller left a famous eyewitness description of the Peacock Throne?
questions.explanation: The French jeweller Jean-Baptiste Tavernier described the Peacock Throne in detail during Shah Jahan's reign.

questions.q: Nadir Shah's seizure of the Peacock Throne followed his victory over the Mughal army at which battle?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah defeated the Mughals at the Battle of Karnal in 1739 before sacking Delhi and taking the throne.

questions.q: Which Mughal emperor was on the throne of Delhi when Nadir Shah invaded and seized the Peacock Throne?
questions.explanation: Muhammad Shah, nicknamed 'Rangila', was the Mughal emperor during Nadir Shah's 1739 invasion.

questions.q: Some accounts say two famous jewels, the Koh-i-Noor and another great gem, adorned the throne. Which red gem is often named?
questions.explanation: The great red spinel known as the Timur Ruby is often linked to the Mughal throne treasures taken by Nadir Shah.

questions.q: The Peacock Throne primarily served as a symbol of which idea?
questions.explanation: The dazzling throne was a potent symbol of the wealth and majesty of Mughal sovereignty.

questions.q: In which century did Shah Jahan have the Peacock Throne made?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan, who reigned 1628 to 1658, had the throne built in the 17th century.

questions.q: After Nadir Shah took it to Persia, what is generally believed to have happened to the original Peacock Throne?
options: It was lost or broken up after his assassination
It was placed in a European museum
It was buried with him intact
questions.explanation: Following Nadir Shah's assassination in 1747, the original throne is believed to have been broken up amid the chaos.

questions.q: The hall of the Red Fort in Delhi that housed the Peacock Throne was known by which name?
questions.explanation: The Peacock Throne stood in the Diwan-i-Khas, the hall of private audience in the Red Fort.

questions.q: Later Persian rulers used the term 'Peacock Throne' to refer to what?
options: The monarchy or throne of Iran itself
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Peacock Throne' came to symbolise the Persian (Iranian) monarchy in later centuries.

questions.q: According to Tavernier's account, the canopy of the throne was supported by columns and topped by figures of peacocks studded with which stones?
options: Coloured gems forming the peacocks' tails
questions.explanation: Tavernier described peacocks whose spread tails were rendered in blue sapphires and other coloured gems set in gold.

questions.q: The famed diamond the Koh-i-Noor, taken with the throne, passed eventually to which power after the annexation of Punjab in 1849?
questions.explanation: After changing many hands following 1739, the Koh-i-Noor went to the British Crown after Punjab's annexation in 1849.

questions.q: Successor Mughal emperors after 1739 used replacement thrones, but these lacked the original's status as what?
questions.explanation: Later thrones were mere substitutes; the irreplaceable original gem-encrusted throne was gone for good after 1739.

questions.q: The vast treasure Nadir Shah extracted from Delhi in 1739 was so great that he reportedly did what for his Persian subjects?
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah's plunder from Delhi was so immense that he is said to have remitted taxes in Persia for several years.

questions.q: The poet and writer Abdul Hamid Lahori, a chronicler of Shah Jahan, recorded the making of which Mughal treasures including the throne in which work?
questions.explanation: The Padshahnama, the official chronicle of Shah Jahan's reign, records the creation of the Peacock Throne.

questions.q: Modern reproductions and later 'Peacock Thrones' in Iran were inspired by the original, but the Qajar-era Naderi throne is sometimes confused with it. What does this show?
options: The original's fame outlived the object itself
questions.explanation: Later Iranian thrones borrowed the prestige of the name, showing how the legend outlasted the lost original.

questions.q: Nadir Shah's invasion that carried off the Peacock Throne is often cited by historians as a key marker of what?
options: The exposure of Mughal military weakness
questions.explanation: The ease of Nadir Shah's 1739 victory laid bare the Mughal Empire's deep decline and military weakness.

questions.q: The original Peacock Throne is reported to have been raised on legs and reached by which feature?
questions.explanation: Contemporary descriptions speak of the throne being ascended by steps, set with gold and gems, befitting its grandeur.
